title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on an already installed RHEL 9 system requires modifying dracut configuration and kernel boot parameters, which is not supported and can render the system unbootable. This change involves bootloader and initramfs modifications, posing a high risk of lockout or system failure.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; FIPS mode activation; kernel parameter change; bootloader configuration; potential system unbootable

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires modifying kernel boot arguments and cryptographic policy, which is not supported on an already installed system and can cause system instability or loss of access. The change is permanent, needs a reboot, and may affect authentication services, making it too risky to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; kernel_parameter_change; fips_mode; potential_system_instability; bootloader_modification

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on an already installed RHEL 9 system requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter, which is unsupported and can render the system unbootable or lock out access. The finding cannot be remedied without reinstalling the OS, making it too risky to apply.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; boot failure; system lockout; FIPS compliance enforcement; kernel parameter change

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: Remediation may require upgrading or migrating the operating system, which can affect the bootloader, kernel, and critical services, introducing a risk of losing SSH/console access or breaking functionality.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; potential loss of SSH access; bootloader or kernel changes; service disruption

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Remediation requires deleting or disabling local user accounts. Mistakenly removing the account used for SSH or console access could lock out administrators, so a human must verify which accounts are truly unauthorized before proceeding.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; authentication; authorization; potential lockout

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: Changing the GRUB2 superuser username modifies bootloader configuration files and requires regenerating the GRUB menu. An incorrect change can prevent the bootloader from loading, leading to system lockout or boot failure, which is a high‑risk operation.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; bootloader configuration; potential boot failure; password protection; system lockout

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: Changing the GRUB2 boot loader password modifies boot loader configuration, which can lock out access if the password is forgotten or misconfigured. According to policy, bootloader changes are classified as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; potential bootloader lockout; loss of remote console access; boot failure if configuration corrupted

title: Disable the use of user namespaces
triage_reason: Changing user.max_user_namespaces to 0 is a sysctl edit, which is generally safe, but it can disrupt container workloads that rely on user namespaces. A review is needed to confirm that no critical containers are running or that alternative configurations are in place.

title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/postfix/main.cf to set smtpd_client_restrictions, which is a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect SSH, sudo, or critical system boot components. The change is reversible and can be tested before production rollout.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; filesystems affected; Potential disruption of mail delivery if local network restrictions are misapplied; Remote mail clients may be unable to send mail until proper relay permissions are configured
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/postfix/main.cf /etc/postfix/main.cf.bak; grep -E -q '^\s*#?\s*smtpd_client_restrictions' /etc/postfix/main.cf; echo 'smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject' >> /etc/postfix/main.cf; systemctl reload postfix; systemctl status postfix; systemctl start postfix
review_feedback: The plan will correctly add or replace the smtpd_client_restrictions line, which addresses the unrestricted relay issue. However, it should reload or restart Postfix after editing and could use postconf -e for a more atomic update. Handling commented lines and backing up the config file would also improve safety.
review_concerns: Postfix is not reloaded after the configuration change, so the new setting may not take effect until a manual restart.; If the parameter is present but commented out, the script will append a new line, leaving the commented line unchanged.; The script does not create a backup of /etc/postfix/main.cf before modifying it.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits the Postfix main configuration to enforce smtpd_client_restrictions, which is the recommended mitigation for unrestricted relay. This change does not affect critical system services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. While the script does not reload Postfix and does not backup the file, these are operational conveniences rather than safety concerns. The modification is limited to a single parameter and will not interrupt other services.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate configuration line if a commented out entry exists; New setting will not take effect until Postfix is reloaded or restarted

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing the Chrony configuration file to ensure only 'server' directives are present and restarting the chronyd service. This is a straightforward change that does not affect authentication, SSH, critical services, or system boot. The risk of lockout or service disruption is minimal.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential time sync disruption if no valid NTP servers are configured
attempt_2_commands: cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -E 's/^\s*(pool|peer|broadcast|ntp)[[:space:]]/# &/' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i -E 's/^#\s*server[[:space:]]/server /' /etc/chrony.conf; chronyd -Q; chronyd -t; chronyd -Q -f /etc/chrony.conf; which chronyd; systemctl restart chronyd; systemctl status chronyd --no-pager; sed -i 's|^#\s*pool.*|server 2.rocky.pool.ntp.org iburst|' /etc/chrony.conf; chronyd -Q -f /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan adds a server directive and validates the configuration before restarting chronyd, which will satisfy the requirement that only server directives are used. However, it lacks a backup step and does not check for existing server entries, which could lead to duplicate lines or make rollback harder.
review_concerns: No backup of /etc/chrony.conf is created before editing.; The plan does not verify that a server directive does not already exist, risking duplicate entries.; Appending the line without checking file permissions may fail if the script is not run with sufficient privileges.
qa_verdict_reason: Adding a server directive to chrony configuration and restarting the service is a standard, low‑risk operation. It does not affect SSH, audit, firewall, or networking services. The only minor risk is duplicate server entries or a brief loss of time sync during restart, which is unlikely to cause critical outages.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate server lines if a server directive already exists; Brief interruption of time synchronization while chronyd restarts; No backup of the original configuration, making rollback slightly harder

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: Enabling a certmap in SSSD is a standard authentication configuration change. It involves editing a single config file and restarting the sssd service, which is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage if performed correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; misconfiguration could prevent user authentication; incorrect syntax may stop sssd service
attempt_3_commands:
tar -czf /etc/sssd/sssd.conf.bak.tar.gz -C /etc/sssd ssd.conf; tar -czf /etc/sssd/sssd.conf.bak.tar.gz /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; dd if=/etc/sssd/sssd.conf of=/etc/sssd/sssd.conf.bak bs=1M status=none; printf '
[certmap/testing.test/rule_name]
matchrule = <SAN>.*EDIPI@mil
maprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin})
domains = testing.test
' >> /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; chmod 600 /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; restorecon -v /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; sssd -t; systemctl reload sssd; systemctl restart sssd; dnf list installed sssd
review_feedback: The plan includes a backup, SELinux,, syntax validation, and a service reload, which should enable the required certmap. However, it does not verify that the target domain exists or prevent duplicate sections, and the backup method could be simplified.
review_concerns: No verification that the domain 'testing.test' is defined in the existing SSSD configuration.; Potential for duplicate certmap sections if the plan is run multiple times.; Using tar for backup may be unnecessary and could be error‑prone compared to a simple cp.; Reload may not apply the new certmap in all environments; a full restart might be required.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan creates a backup, validates the configuration before applying, restores SELinux context, and reloads SSSD rather than restarting it. These steps minimize risk of service disruption. Potential issues such as duplicate sections or missing domain are mitigated by the syntax test (sssd -t) before reload, making the remediation unlikely to lock out authentication or break critical services.
qa_side_effects: Possible duplicate certmap section if the plan is executed multiple times; If the domain 'testing.test' is not defined, the new section will be ignored but will not cause a failure due to pre‑validation
